1: צַדִּיק אַתָּה יְהוָה (tzaddiq attah YHWH): Jeremiah opens his theodicy complaint by formally acknowledging YHWH's righteousness — the legal premise of the lawsuit (rib). אַךְ מִשְׁפָּטִים אֲדַבֵּר אוֹתָךְ (akh mishpatim adabber otakha, yet I will argue my case with you): mishpatim are legal judgments; the prophetic complaint is a formal legal proceeding. The complaint: why does the wicked prosper (shalehu, be undisturbed)?
2: נְטַעְתָּם גַּם-שֹׁרָשׁוּ (neta'tam gam-shorrashu, you planted them and they have taken root): YHWH's own planting is acknowledged. The devastating diagnosis: קָרוֹב בְּפִיהֶם וְרָחוֹק מִכִּלְיוֹתֵיהֶם (qarov befiyhem verachok mikillyotehem, near in their mouths but far from their inner being) — verbal religion without inner reality. Cf. Isa 29:13; Matt 15:8.
3: YHWH knows (yada'ta) Jeremiah intimately — the covenantal knowledge that distinguishes genuine relationship from verbal profession. בָּחַנְתָּ לִבִּי (vachanta libbi, you have tested my heart): YHWH's scrutiny confirms genuine covenant loyalty. הַשְׁלִיכֵם כְּצֹאן לְטִבְחָה (hashlikhem ketzon letivchah): the lamb-imagery of ch 11 reversed — the persecutors become the ones marked for slaughter.
4: עַד-מָתַי תֶּאֱבַל הָאָרֶץ (ad-matai te'eval ha'aretz, how long will the land mourn): the land participates in covenant consequences — drought and famine as manifestations of broken covenant (Deut 28:23-24). The wicked claim: 'He will not see our latter end' — denying divine judgment.
5: YHWH's unexpected response: כִּי אֶת-רַגְלִים רַצְתָּ (ki et-raglim ratztah, if you have raced against foot-soldiers): a challenge-question that escalates stakes. If this level of difficulty wears Jeremiah out, how will he survive the thicket of the Jordan? The point: what is coming will be harder, not easier; the prophet must deepen rather than seek relief.
6: Even Jeremiah's own brothers (acheycha) have dealt treacherously (bagadu) with him. אַל-תַּאֲמֵן בָּהֶם (al-ta'amen bahem, do not trust them): the root aman — do not place emunah in them. Micah 7:5-6 captures the same family-betrayal dynamic; Jesus invokes it in Matt 10:35-36.
7: עָזַבְתִּי אֶת-בֵּיתִי (azavti et-beiti, I have forsaken my house): YHWH speaks as one who has abandoned his own household — temple and covenantal family. נָתַתִּי אֶת-יְדִידוּת נַפְשִׁי (natati et-yedidut nafshi, I have given the beloved of my soul): yedidut is passionate covenant-love. Giving her over to enemies is an act of grief-driven self-sacrifice.
8: My inheritance (nachalati) has become like a lion roaring against me — the covenant relationship that should be the greatest good has become the source of the greatest grief. The lion that should be a lamb has turned predatory.
9: Is my inheritance like a speckled bird of prey surrounded by other birds of prey? The image suggests Israel's exposed, vulnerable position — made a target by the surrounding nations because of her unfaithfulness.
10: רֹעִים רַבִּים שִׁחֲתוּ כַרְמִי (ro'im rabbim shichatu karmi, many shepherds have ruined my vineyard): kerem (vineyard) is the covenant metaphor for Israel (cf. Isa 5:1-7). The shepherd-rulers who trample the vineyard stand condemned — the sequence continues through Ezek 34 and John 10.
11: שְׁמָמָה (shemamah, desolation): the land mourning (avelah) before YHWH. Because no one lays it to heart (sheym al lev, takes it seriously), the desolation persists.
12: Destroyers have come upon all the bare heights in the wilderness — YHWH's sword devours from one end to the other. No peace (shalom) for any flesh — the sword is a covenant curse for treaty-breaking (Lev 26:25).
13: זָרְעוּ חִטִּים וְקֹצִים קָצָרוּ (zar'u chittim veqotzim qatzaru, they sowed wheat but reap thorns): the agricultural reversal of covenant blessing (Deut 28:18-24) — futile labor as covenant curse. Ashamed (hivoshu) of their produce because of YHWH's fierce anger.
14: The nations: evil neighbors who touch Israel's inheritance will be uprooted (attesh) from their land. But after uprooting, YHWH will have compassion — the first hint that judgment on nations serves a larger restorative purpose.
15: וְשַׁבְתִּי וְרִחַמְתִּים (veshavti verichhamtim, I will return and have compassion on them): the compassion-after-judgment pattern that drives the new covenant promise. Each person will be brought back to their heritage (nachlatohu) and land.
16: Nations who learn Israel's ways — who learn to swear by YHWH's name rather than Baal — will be built up (yibbaneh) among YHWH's people. Covenant membership is extendable to nations who adopt the covenant pattern — anticipating the Gentile inclusion of the NT.
17: The nation that does not listen (lo yishma') will be utterly uprooted and destroyed — the same covenant-obedience standard applies to all nations. Covenant relationship brings both the promise of inclusion and the threat of consequence.
